label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the Indians believed according to the letter they wrote. The letter was in response to an invitation to send their boys to William and Mary College. First, I need to understand the content of the letter. The main points are: the Indians acknowledge the offer, thank the officials for their good intentions, but refuse because they have a different view on education. They mention that some of their young people went to northern colleges, but when they returned, they were "bad manners" and "good for nothing" in terms of living in the woods. Then they offer to educate the officials' sons in their own way. The question is asking what the Indians believed. Let's look at the options: (A) They thought it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling. But the letter says they refused the offer, so maybe not A. The problem is they thought the education would make the boys unfit for their way of life. (B) They felt insulted by the offer. The letter starts by saying they know the officials meant well, and they thank them, so probably not B. They don't seem insulted, just have a different view. (C) They knew more science than the officials. The letter says that the officials' colleges taught all their sciences, but the Indians' education is different. The Indians don't claim to know more science, but that they have a different way of living. So probably not C. (D) They had a better way of educating young men. The letter implies that their way of education is more suited to their needs, and they even offer to take the officials' sons to teach them
